32:
"We will pass over armed before the LORD into the land of Canaan, that the possession of our inheritance on this side Jordan may be ours." The witnesses note the pledge sealed once more — they would pass over armed before the LORD, that their east-Jordan inheritance be secured. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is repeated again and again, for confirmation, assuring it should be strictly performed. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the pledge — pass over armed before the LORD, that the possession on this side Jordan be theirs. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the pass over armed — crossing into Canaan; the before the LORD — in his service; the our inheritance ours — the east-Jordan possession secured; the repeated for confirmation — the word made sure, the performance certain.

The witnesses agree the repeated pledging seals the covenant beyond doubt: the two tribes bound themselves yet again to cross armed before the LORD, that their inheritance might be lawfully theirs; Gill marks how often the promise is rehearsed, for the confirming of it — a solemn earnestness that their word should be sure and their performance certain, the inheritance to be held not by mere desire but by the faithful keeping of their covenant, the gift secured by the duty fulfilled.

So the witnesses make the verse the pledge sealed: The witnesses note the pass over armed: crossing into Canaan. The witnesses note the before the LORD: in his service. The witnesses note the our inheritance ours: the east-Jordan possession secured. The witnesses note the repeated for confirmation: the word made sure, the performance certain. The expositors observe the pass over armed: crossing into Canaan. The expositors observe the before the LORD: in his service. The expositors observe the our inheritance ours: the east-Jordan possession secured. The expositors observe the repeated for confirmation: the word made sure, the performance certain. The verse thus records that they pledged again to pass over armed before the LORD, that their inheritance be theirs — the covenant sealed beyond doubt, the gift secured by the duty faithfully kept.
33:
"And Moses gave unto them, even to the children of Gad, and to the children of Reuben, and unto half the tribe of Manasseh... the kingdom of Sihon king of the Amorites, and the kingdom of Og king of Bashan..." The witnesses note the grant made — the kingdoms of Sihon and Og given to Gad, Reuben, and now half Manasseh. The witnesses note that Gill notes Moses gave it by word in the court, or by some instrument drawn up and signed by him and the sanhedrim. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes this is the first mention of Manasseh, the original application having been only by Gad and Reuben; half Manasseh is now joined to them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses gave — by formal, witnessed deed; the Gad, Reuben, half Manasseh — the two tribes and a half; the Sihon and Og — the conquered kingdoms granted; the Manasseh added — the portion enlarged to fill the land won.

The witnesses agree the grant formally settled the great east-Jordan territory upon the two tribes and a half: the conquered kingdoms of Sihon and Og were given them as a possession, and half Manasseh, not at first a party to the request, was now added to share the land; the joining of Manasseh shows the portion enlarged to fill the country won, the whole region of the Amorite kings parted among these tribes by Moses' formal and witnessed deed.

So the witnesses make the verse the grant: The witnesses note the Moses gave: by formal, witnessed deed. The witnesses note the Gad, Reuben, half Manasseh: the two tribes and a half. The witnesses note the Sihon and Og: the conquered kingdoms granted. The witnesses note the Manasseh added: the portion enlarged to fill the land won. The expositors observe the Moses gave: by formal, witnessed deed. The expositors observe the Gad, Reuben, half Manasseh: the two tribes and a half. The expositors observe the Sihon and Og: the conquered kingdoms granted. The expositors observe the Manasseh added: the portion enlarged to fill the land won. The verse thus records that Moses granted the kingdoms of Sihon and Og to Gad, Reuben, and half Manasseh — the east-Jordan land formally settled, Manasseh added to fill the conquered country.
34:
"And the children of Gad built Dibon, and Ataroth, and Aroer," The witnesses note the Gadites building — restoring and fortifying — Dibon, Ataroth, and Aroer. The witnesses note that Gill notes they rather repaired the walls and rebuilt houses demolished in the wars with Sihon and Og. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the Gadites restoring and fortifying Dibon, Ataroth, and Aroer. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer — the towns the Gadites built; the restored and fortified — repaired, not raised anew; the after the wars — demolished under Sihon and Og; the settling their portion — making the towns fit for their families.

The witnesses agree the building of these cities was in truth a repairing and fortifying of towns already standing: the Gadites did not raise new cities, for which there was neither time nor need, but restored and walled the places taken from Sihon and Og, making them fit for their families and flocks; the quiet record of the work shows the two tribes settling into the portion granted them, the promise kept on God's side as they prepared to keep theirs.

So the witnesses make the verse the cities built: The witnesses note the Dibon, Ataroth, Aroer: the towns the Gadites built. The witnesses note the restored and fortified: repaired, not raised anew. The witnesses note the after the wars: demolished under Sihon and Og. The witnesses note the settling their portion: making the towns fit for their families. The expositors observe the after the wars: demolished under Sihon and Og. The expositors observe the settling their portion: making the towns fit for their families. The verse thus records that the Gadites built Dibon, Ataroth, and Aroer — repairing the towns taken from Sihon and Og, settling into their granted portion.
35:
"And Atroth, Shophan, and Jaazer, and Jogbehah," The witnesses note further Gadite cities — Atroth, Shophan, Jaazer, and Jogbehah. The witnesses note that Gill notes of these no mention is made elsewhere except Jaazer, the same with Jazer. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch continue the list of Gadite towns. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Atroth, Shophan — further Gadite towns; the Jaazer and Jogbehah — among the cities built; the Jaazer the same as Jazer — the one named elsewhere; the repaired, not raised anew — the towns restored after the wars; the the list continued — the Gadite portion filled with fortified towns.

36:
"And Bethnimrah, and Bethharan, fenced cities: and folds for sheep." The witnesses note the Gadites building Bethnimrah and Bethharan as fenced cities, with folds for their sheep. The witnesses note that Gill notes Bethnimrah is the same as Nimrah, and Bethharan the same as Betharam, later called Livias or Julias by Herod; these the Gadites built or repaired for their families. The witnesses note that they were fenced cities, with sheepfolds. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Bethnimrah and Bethharan — the same as Nimrah and Betharam; the fenced cities — walled for safety; the folds for sheep — provision for the flocks; the keeping their plan — securing family and substance before the war.

The witnesses agree the building of fenced cities and folds fulfilled the very provision the tribes had proposed: walled towns for the safety of their little ones and folds for the flocks, exactly as they had said to Moses; the orderly settling of the Gadites — securing both family and substance before going to the war — shows them keeping faith with their own plan, providing for those they left behind while they themselves prepared to cross Jordan and fight.

So the witnesses make the verse the fenced cities: The witnesses note the Bethnimrah and Bethharan: the same as Nimrah and Betharam. The witnesses note the fenced cities: walled for safety. The witnesses note the folds for sheep: provision for the flocks. The witnesses note the keeping their plan: securing family and substance before the war. The expositors observe the folds for sheep: provision for the flocks. The expositors observe the keeping their plan: securing family and substance before the war. 37:
"And the children of Reuben built Heshbon, and Elealeh, and Kirjathaim," The witnesses note the Reubenites building Heshbon, Elealeh, and Kirjathaim — old cities repaired, not raised anew. The witnesses note that Gill notes Heshbon was the royal city of Sihon, and Elealeh within a mile of it; these were cities long before, only repaired, for they had not time to build new, passing over Jordan in a few months. The witnesses note that the Reubenites built these for their portion. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Heshbon, Elealeh, Kirjathaim — the Reubenite towns; the Heshbon the royal city — of Sihon king of the Amorites; the old cities repaired — not raised anew, for lack of time; the settling before Jordan — within a few months of crossing.

The witnesses agree the building of these cities was in truth a repairing and fortifying of towns already standing: the Reubenites did not raise new cities, for which there was neither time nor need, but restored and walled the places taken from Sihon and Og, making them fit for their families and flocks; the quiet record of the work shows the two tribes settling into the portion granted them, the promise kept on God's side as they prepared to keep theirs.

So the witnesses make the verse the cities built: The witnesses note the Heshbon, Elealeh, Kirjathaim: the Reubenite towns. The witnesses note the Heshbon the royal city: of Sihon king of the Amorites. The witnesses note the old cities repaired: not raised anew, for lack of time. The witnesses note the settling before Jordan: within a few months of crossing. The expositors observe the Heshbon, Elealeh, Kirjathaim: the Reubenite towns. 38:
"And Nebo, and Baalmeon, (their names being changed,) and Shibmah: and gave other names unto the cities which they builded." The witnesses note the Reubenites changing the idolatrous names of Nebo and Baalmeon, giving the cities other names. The witnesses note that Gill notes Nebo was the name of an idol, and Baalmeon means "Baal's habitation," likely a temple of his; the Reubenites, not liking to retain such idolatrous names, gave them others. The witnesses note that the new names are not recorded. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Nebo, Baalmeon — named after idols; the their names changed — the Reubenites refusing to keep them; the other names given — purging the idol-names away; the separation from idols — not so much as naming false gods (Exod. 23:13).

The witnesses agree the changing of the names was an act of pious separation from idolatry: the Reubenites would not keep on their cities the names of false gods — Nebo the idol, Baalmeon the dwelling of Baal — but gave them other names, refusing to honour idols even in speech; the small but telling act shows a right zeal to purge away every relic of false worship, that the people of God might not so much as name the idols of the heathen, but put away the very memory of them (cf. Exod. 23:13, make no mention of the name of other gods).

So the witnesses make the verse the names changed: The witnesses note the Nebo, Baalmeon: named after idols. The witnesses note the their names changed: the Reubenites refusing to keep them. The witnesses note the other names given: purging the idol-names away. The witnesses note the separation from idols: not so much as naming false gods (Exod. 23:13). The expositors observe the other names given: purging the idol-names away. The expositors observe the separation from idols: not so much as naming false gods (Exod. 23:13). 39:
"And the children of Machir the son of Manasseh went to Gilead, and took it, and dispossessed the Amorite which was in it." The witnesses note the children of Machir taking that part of Gilead still held by the Amorites. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the part of Gilead Moses gave to half Manasseh, till now in the hand of the Amorites; half Mount Gilead and its cities had been given to Reuben and Gad, the part already conquered. The witnesses note that Machir's children dispossessed the Amorites there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the children of Machir — the sons of Manasseh; the went to Gilead — the part still unconquered; the took it — seizing the land by force; the dispossessed the Amorite — their portion won as well as granted.

The witnesses agree the Machirites earned their portion by their own conquest: the part of Gilead still in Amorite hands they took by force, dispossessing its inhabitants, so that their inheritance was won as well as granted; the valour of Manasseh's sons, going up to seize the land yet unsubdued, marks them as worthy of their portion, the territory possessed not idly but by the same warlike effort that had won the rest of the country for Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse Machir takes Gilead: The witnesses note the the children of Machir: the sons of Manasseh. The witnesses note the went to Gilead: the part still unconquered. The witnesses note the took it: seizing the land by force. The witnesses note the dispossessed the Amorite: their portion won as well as granted. The expositors observe the the children of Machir: the sons of Manasseh. The expositors observe the went to Gilead: the part still unconquered. The expositors observe the took it: seizing the land by force. The expositors observe the dispossessed the Amorite: their portion won as well as granted. The verse thus records that the Machirites took the Amorite part of Gilead — their inheritance won by their own conquest, the valour of Manasseh's sons making them worthy of their portion.
40:
"And Moses gave Gilead unto Machir the son of Manasseh; and he dwelt therein." The witnesses note Moses giving Gilead to the children of Machir, who then dwelt there. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was to the children of Machir who went and took it; some think Machir himself was alive, but that is not probable, for all over twenty at the Exodus died in the wilderness save Caleb and Joshua. The witnesses note that they dwelt in the land they had taken. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses gave Gilead — confirming the conquest by grant; the to the children of Machir — the new generation, not the dead father; the he dwelt therein — settling the land they had won; the the promise to the sons — the fathers fallen, the children entering in.

The witnesses agree the grant confirmed to the Machirites what their valour had won: Moses gave them Gilead for a possession, and they settled in it — the gift of the leader sealing the conquest of their hands; Gill's note that Machir himself was surely dead (the whole generation having perished save the two faithful) reminds us that it was the children, the new generation, who entered into the inheritance, the fathers fallen and the sons settling the land, as throughout this book the promise passes to those who go in.

So the witnesses make the verse Gilead given: The witnesses note the Moses gave Gilead: confirming the conquest by grant. The witnesses note the to the children of Machir: the new generation, not the dead father. The witnesses note the he dwelt therein: settling the land they had won. The witnesses note the the promise to the sons: the fathers fallen, the children entering in. The expositors observe the Moses gave Gilead: confirming the conquest by grant. The expositors observe the to the children of Machir: the new generation, not the dead father. The expositors observe the he dwelt therein: settling the land they had won. The expositors observe the the promise to the sons: the fathers fallen, the children entering in. 41:
"And Jair the son of Manasseh went and took the small towns thereof, and called them Havoth-jair." The witnesses note Jair taking the small towns and naming them Havoth-jair, the villages of Jair. The witnesses note that Gill notes Jair was of Manasseh by his mother's side, but of Judah by descent, through Hezron who married a daughter of Machir. The witnesses note that Clarke renders Havoth-jair "the villages or habitations of Jair," and notes these tribes lay east of Jordan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Jair took the towns — the small towns of the region; the called them Havoth-jair — the villages of Jair; the of Judah and Manasseh — the tribes mingled in his line; the a memorial — his conquest named for remembrance.

The witnesses agree the naming of the towns after their conqueror marks both the valour and the memorial of Jair: he took the small towns of the region and called them by his own name, "the villages of Jair," that his conquest be remembered; the note that Jair sprang of Judah and Manasseh joined shows the mingling of the tribes in the line, and the settling of the east-Jordan land by the energy of such men, who took their portion and left their name upon it for a memorial in Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse Havoth-jair: The witnesses note the Jair took the towns: the small towns of the region. The witnesses note the called them Havoth-jair: the villages of Jair. The witnesses note the of Judah and Manasseh: the tribes mingled in his line. The witnesses note the a memorial: his conquest named for remembrance. The expositors observe the Jair took the towns: the small towns of the region. The expositors observe the called them Havoth-jair: the villages of Jair. The expositors observe the of Judah and Manasseh: the tribes mingled in his line. The expositors observe the a memorial: his conquest named for remembrance. 42:
"And Nobah went and took Kenath, and the villages thereof, and called it Nobah, after his own name." The witnesses note Nobah taking Kenath and its villages, naming it after himself. The witnesses note that Gill notes who Nobah was is uncertain, very probably a descendant of Manasseh, born in Egypt, who died after Moses and was buried beyond Jordan, as did Machir and Jair, so that none of that generation were left but Caleb and Joshua. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Nobah taking Kenath and calling it by his name. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Nobah took Kenath — the city and its villages; the called it Nobah — after his own name; the a descendant of Manasseh — born in Egypt, dying beyond Jordan; the the generation passing — none left save Caleb and Joshua.

The witnesses agree the chapter closes with another conqueror settling and naming his portion: Nobah took Kenath and called it after himself, like Jair before him, the east-Jordan land parcelled out among the men who won it; and Gill's closing note — that Nobah, Machir, and Jair all died beyond Jordan, none of the old generation left save Caleb and Joshua — sounds again the great theme of these chapters, that the wilderness generation passed away and a new one entered the inheritance, the land settled by the children of those who had fallen.

The witnesses note the a descendant of Manasseh: born in Egypt, dying beyond Jordan. The witnesses note the the generation passing: none left save Caleb and Joshua.
